one_line_takeaway: A black-box (API-only) dataset watermarking scheme that embeds detectable word-pair co-occurrence signals via rephrasing and proves a distribution-free false-positive bound, outperforming prior open-model-only watermarking baselines (STAMP, Radioactive) under closed access and under light text perturbations, while preserving benchmark utility and semantics better than KGW-based rephrasing.
key_finding: In the vanilla (fully contaminated) fine-tuning setting, the proposed method achieves statistically significant detection (p<0.01) across both models and all three benchmarks under closed-model access (e.g., LLaMA-3-8B: MMLU p=8.40e-5, ARC-C p=1.34e-6, ARC-E p=4.11e-6; Gemma-2-2B: MMLU p=4.25e-3, ARC-C p=1.12e-6, ARC-E p=4.81e-5), while the Radioactive baseline under closed-model access fails to detect (p in the 0.2-0.3 range) -- the paper's headline result that black-box detection is only reliable with their method. Under a data-mixture setting where watermarked tokens are only ~1.1-1.3% of fine-tuning tokens, detection remains significant for both models across all three datasets; at a more extreme ~0.5% contamination ratio, LLaMA-3-8B remains detectable but Gemma-2-2B fails to detect on MMLU (p=0.798) and is only marginal on ARC-C/ARC-E. Under lightweight text perturbations (random deletion, synonym substitution, emoji insertion at r=0.2-0.3), the method degrades far less than Radioactive-open, e.g. on MMLU Radioactive's p-value rises from 7.94e-9 (vanilla) to above 0.01 after any of the three 20% perturbations, whereas the proposed method stays at or below ~3.4e-2 in the worst case. Utility/semantic-preservation experiments (Table 4, lm-evaluation-harness across 5 base models: Pythia-1B, Gemma-2-2B, LLaMA-3.2-3B, Qwen-2.5-7B, LLaMA-3-8B) show accuracy on watermarked benchmarks tracks the original within a few points and preserves model ranking; Table 5 shows the method achieves higher SBERT semantic-similarity and substantially higher n-gram lexical overlap than KGW-based rephrasing (e.g., MMLU: SBERT 0.897 vs 0.848, 3-gram 0.643 vs 0.193, 4-gram 0.557 vs 0.121), attributed to localized edits versus KGW's full-sentence rewrites. An empirical false-positive check (100 random secret keys against text generated by the two base models, no watermarking present) observed zero false positives, though the authors note a gap between this empirical rate and the theoretical (looser) upper bound.
core_contribution: The paper introduces the first dataset watermarking method for detecting unauthorized LLM training on a dataset under closed/black-box model access (API-only, no logits or next-token probabilities). The embedding stage rephrases a dataset (using ChatGPT) to raise the co-occurrence frequency of a randomly chosen set of word pairs (the secret key), inducing controlled spurious lexical correlations. The detection stage queries the target model for generated text and computes a statistic comparing co-occurrence deviation (relative to a reference/non-watermarked dataset) for the secret-key word pairs versus all other word pairs; a threshold test on this detection score flags contamination. The authors derive Theorem 4.1, a distribution-free, model-agnostic upper bound on the detector's false positive rate (e^{-2t'^2 d}, via Hoeffding's inequality for sampling without replacement from a hypergeometric distribution), which yields a closed-form p-value.
threat_model: Auditing/IP-protection setting rather than an adversarial-attacker setting: a data owner (auditor) wants to determine, after the fact, whether a model developer trained on their released benchmark/dataset. The auditor has only closed/API access to the trained model (can query and observe generated text, not logits or weights) -- this is explicitly contrasted with prior work (STAMP, Radioactive/KGW-based methods) that assumes open-model access to log-probabilities. The paper's own Limitation section states the method is NOT designed to be robust to a fully adversarial data transformer -- it explicitly notes prior work has shown strong watermarking guarantees are impossible under arbitrary adversarial rewriting of the data, and the method only applies to datasets not yet released (cannot be applied retroactively).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) the method only applies to datasets not yet released, since embedding requires rephrasing before release -- it cannot be retrofitted onto already-published data (shared with all prior watermarking approaches); (2) it is an auditing tool, not a defense -- it does not aim to be robust against fully adversarial/arbitrary rewriting of the data, and the authors cite prior theoretical work showing strong watermarking guarantees are impossible under arbitrary rewriting; (3) due to compute constraints, evaluation is restricted to the post-training/fine-tuning stage; effectiveness at pretraining scale is left as future work; (4) evaluation is conducted on open-weight models (LLaMA-3, Gemma-2) fine-tuned locally because closed commercial models do not permit fine-tuning access -- so the closed-model claim is validated by simulating closed access on open models rather than testing against an actual closed commercial API; (5) the Radioactive baseline could not be evaluated on Gemma-2-2B because it assumes aligned tokenization between the rephrasing model and target model, which does not hold for Gemma (marked N/A in Table 1); (6) detectability is sensitive to hyperparameters (secret-key size, rephrasing rounds, tau) with a U-shaped/trade-off behavior against semantic preservation, requiring tuning per dataset.

datasets_used: 20K and 50K OpenReview paper abstracts (used as diluting/mixture data for the partial-contamination experiment)
quantitative_results: Vanilla setting p-values, closed access, LLaMA-3-8B: MMLU 8.40e-5, ARC-C 1.34e-6, ARC-E 4.11e-6 (Table 1)
Vanilla setting p-values, closed access, Gemma-2-2B: MMLU 4.25e-3, ARC-C 1.12e-6, ARC-E 4.81e-5 (Table 1)
Radioactive closed-access baseline p-values (fails to detect): LLaMA-3-8B MMLU 0.316, ARC-C 0.217, ARC-E 0.216 (Table 1)
Data-mixture (1.3% contamination) p-values: LLaMA MMLU 6.16e-3, ARC-C 1.07e-3, ARC-E 1.77e-3; Gemma MMLU 2.66e-2, ARC-C 1.02e-3, ARC-E 1.77e-2 (Table 2)
Data-mixture (0.5% contamination) p-values: LLaMA MMLU 3.46e-2, ARC-C 2.08e-3, ARC-E 4.48e-3; Gemma MMLU 7.98e-1 (fails), ARC-C 1.26e-1, ARC-E 9.45e-2 (Table 2)

Text-perturbation robustness (MMLU, r=0.2): Ours vanilla 4.80e-6 -> deletion 1.54e-2, synonym 3.35e-2, emoji 9.64e-3; Radioactive-open vanilla 7.94e-9 -> deletion 1.58e-2, synonym 4.61e-2, emoji 5.39e-2 (Table 3)
Semantic/lexical preservation (MMLU): SBERT 0.897 (ours) vs 0.848 (KGW); 3-gram overlap 0.643 vs 0.193; 4-gram overlap 0.557 vs 0.121 (Table 5)

Contamination token ratios (data mixture with 20K/50K OpenReview abstracts): MMLU 64,148 watermarked tokens = 1.284%/0.548%; ARC-C 64,708 tokens = 1.295%/0.552%; ARC-E 57,205 tokens = 1.147%/0.489% (Table 6)
Theorem 4.1 false-positive bound example: for secret-key size d=500 and vocabulary |V|=1000, to target FPR 1e-3 the required threshold is t~0.0832
Empirical false-positive check: zero false positives observed across 100 randomly sampled secret keys on two base models' generated text
baselines_compared: STAMP (Rastogi et al. 2025) -- multiple KGW-rephrased dataset versions, paired t-test on perplexity between public and private versions

Radioactive / watermark radioactivity (Sander et al. 2024, 2025) -- KGW green/red-list token watermarking of the dataset via rephrasing, evaluated in both open-model (next-token green-list bias test) and closed-model (generated-output based) detection variants
